--- title: "5a. Creating Ternary Plot with R" author: "Lei Yuhe" date: "May 14, 2026" date-modified: "last-modified" execute: eval: true echo: true warning: false freeze: true --- ## Packages Two main packages are used: - [`ggtern`](http://www.ggtern.com/): extends `ggplot2` to support ternary coordinate systems - [`plotly`](https://plotly.com/r/): creates interactive web-based charts, its `scatterternary` trace type renders interactive ternary diagrams Supporting tidyverse packages (`readr`, `dplyr`, `tidyr`) handle data import and wrangling. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pacman::p_load(plotly, ggtern, tidyverse) ``` ::: > **Note:** `ggtern` masks several `ggplot2` functions (e.g. `annotate()`). Load it after tidyverse to avoid conflicts, and reload `ggplot2` functions with `ggplot2::` prefix if needed later. ## Data Preparation ### The Dataset **Singapore Residents by Planning Area/Subzone, Age Group, Sex and Type of Dwelling, June 2000-2018** Source: [SingStat](https://www.singstat.gov.sg/find-data/explore-data-themes/population/geographic-distribution/latest-news-data) File: `data/respopagsex2000to2018_tidy.csv` | Column | Description | |--------|-------------| | `PA` | Planning Area | | `SZ` | Subzone | | `AG` | Age Group | | `Sex` | Sex | | `TOD` | Type of Dwelling | | `Population` | Resident count | | `Year` | Reference year (2011 - 2020) | ### Import Data ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pop_data <- read_csv("data/respopagsex2000to2018_tidy.csv") glimpse(pop_data) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 108,126 Columns: 5 $ PA "Ang Mo Kio", "Ang Mo Kio", "Ang Mo Kio", "Ang Mo Kio", "An… $ SZ "Ang Mo Kio Town Centre", "Ang Mo Kio Town Centre", "Ang Mo… $ AG "AGE0-4", "AGE0-4", "AGE0-4", "AGE0-4", "AGE0-4", "AGE0-4",… $ Year 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, 2011, 2012,… $ Population 290, 270, 260, 250, 260, 250, 200, 180, 290, 290, 270, 300,… ``` ::: ::: ### Derive Age-Group Aggregates Three composite measures are derived by summing across age-group columns after pivoting wide: | Measure | Age bands included | |---------|--------------------| | `YOUNG` | 0–4, 5–9, 10–14, 15–19, 20–24 (columns 4–8) | | `ACTIVE` | 25–29, … , 55–59, 60–64 (columns 9–16) | | `OLD` | 65–69, 70–74, 75–79, 80–84, 85+ (columns 17–21) | ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} #Deriving the young, economy active and old measures agpop_mutated <- pop_data %>% mutate(`Year` = as.character(Year))%>% spread(AG, Population) %>% mutate(YOUNG = rowSums(.[4:8]))%>% mutate(ACTIVE = rowSums(.[9:16])) %>% mutate(OLD = rowSums(.[17:21])) %>% mutate(TOTAL = rowSums(.[22:24])) %>% filter(Year == 2018)%>% filter(TOTAL > 0) glimpse(agpop_mutated) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 234 Columns: 25 $ PA "Ang Mo Kio", "Ang Mo Kio", "Ang Mo Kio", "Ang Mo Kio", "An… $ SZ "Ang Mo Kio Town Centre", "Cheng San", "Chong Boon", "Kebun… $ Year "2018", "2018", "2018", "2018", "2018", "2018", "2018", "20… $ `AGE0-4` 180, 1060, 900, 720, 220, 550, 260, 830, 160, 810, 350, 282… $ `AGE05-9` 270, 1080, 900, 850, 310, 630, 340, 930, 160, 1070, 460, 32… $ `AGE10-14` 320, 1080, 1030, 1010, 380, 670, 430, 930, 220, 1300, 490, … $ `AGE15-19` 300, 1260, 1220, 1120, 500, 780, 500, 860, 260, 1450, 400, … $ `AGE20-24` 260, 1400, 1380, 1230, 550, 950, 640, 1020, 350, 1500, 330,… $ `AGE25-29` 300, 1880, 1760, 1460, 500, 1080, 690, 1400, 340, 1590, 310… $ `AGE30-34` 270, 1940, 1830, 1330, 300, 990, 440, 1350, 230, 1390, 310,… $ `AGE35-39` 330, 2300, 1920, 1540, 290, 1100, 400, 1700, 250, 1770, 630… $ `AGE40-44` 430, 2090, 1900, 1700, 420, 1140, 490, 1720, 260, 1860, 810… $ `AGE45-49` 470, 2180, 1910, 1830, 550, 1230, 580, 1530, 320, 2000, 830… $ `AGE50-54` 360, 2160, 2070, 1880, 550, 1350, 640, 1480, 300, 1980, 620… $ `AGE55-59` 310, 2150, 2140, 1810, 560, 1420, 730, 1720, 360, 2010, 460… $ `AGE60-64` 300, 2270, 2170, 1750, 450, 1290, 680, 1680, 350, 1980, 390… $ `AGE65-69` 270, 2130, 2050, 1700, 410, 1150, 500, 1610, 250, 1790, 340… $ `AGE70-74` 190, 1370, 1570, 1240, 290, 830, 280, 1190, 160, 1090, 220,… $ `AGE75-79` 150, 980, 1170, 870, 220, 680, 210, 980, 100, 690, 110, 257… $ `AGE80-84` 60, 560, 640, 540, 140, 360, 180, 560, 70, 390, 80, 1520, 2… $ AGE85over 60, 440, 530, 430, 140, 340, 130, 460, 60, 310, 100, 1350, … $ YOUNG 1330, 5880, 5430, 4930, 1960, 3580, 2170, 4570, 1150, 6130,… $ ACTIVE 2770, 16970, 15700, 13300, 3620, 9600, 4650, 12580, 2410, 1… $ OLD 730, 5480, 5960, 4780, 1200, 3360, 1300, 4800, 640, 4270, 8… $ TOTAL 4830, 28330, 27090, 23010, 6780, 16540, 8120, 21950, 4200, … ``` ::: ::: > **Add-on tip:** To make the workflow more robust, replace column-index slicing (`.[4:8]`) with explicit column name ranges using `dplyr::across()` once you have verified the column order. --- ## Plotting Ternary Diagrams ### Static Ternary Plot - Minimal `ggtern()` follows the same grammar as `ggplot2` but uses a ternary coordinate system with axes `x`, `y`, and `z`. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggtern(data = agpop_mutated, aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) + geom_point() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Basic static ternary plot — population structure by subzone, 2018](Hands-on_Ex05a_files/figure-html/static-basic-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ### Static Ternary Plot - Styled Adding a title and the `theme_rgbw()` theme colours the axes Red / Green / Blue / White for easier reading. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggtern(data = agpop_mutated, aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 2, colour = "steelblue") + labs(title = "Population Structure by Subzone, 2018", subtitle = "Singapore Residents — Young / Active / Old composition", x = "Young", y = "Active", z = "Old") + theme_rgbw() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Styled static ternary plot with theme_rgbw()](Hands-on_Ex05a_files/figure-html/static-styled-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: #### Add-on: Colour-encode a grouping variable {.unnumbered} If planning areas (`PA`) are available after the spread, you can map colour to a categorical variable to reveal spatial clusters: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggtern(data = agpop_mutated, aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD, colour = PA)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) + labs(title = "Population Structure by Planning Area, 2018") + theme_rgbw() + theme(legend.position = "right") ``` ::: ### Interactive Ternary Plot with Plotly `plot_ly()` with `type = "scatterternary"` renders an interactive version where you can hover to inspect individual subzones. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Helper: annotation label label <- function(txt) { list( text = txt, x = 0.1, y = 1, ax = 0, ay = 0, xref = "paper", yref = "paper", align = "center", font = list(family = "serif", size = 15, color = "white"), bgcolor = "#b3b3b3", bordercolor = "black", borderwidth = 2 ) } # Helper: axis formatting axis <- function(txt) { list(title = txt, tickformat = ".0%", tickfont = list(size = 10)) } ternaryAxes <- list( aaxis = axis("Young"), baxis = axis("Active"), caxis = axis("Old") ) plot_ly( agpop_mutated, a = ~YOUNG, b = ~ACTIVE, c = ~OLD, color = I("steelblue"), type = "scatterternary", mode = "markers", text = ~paste("Subzone:", SZ, "
Total:", TOTAL), # tooltip add-on hoverinfo = "text" ) %>% layout( title = "Population Structure by Subzone, Singapore 2018", annotations = label("Ternary Markers"), ternary = ternaryAxes ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive ternary plot — hover to see subzone details ::: ::: #### Add-on: Colour by population size {.unnumbered} Map marker colour to `TOTAL` population for a richer visual encoding: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plot_ly( agpop_mutated, a = ~YOUNG, b = ~ACTIVE, c = ~OLD, color = ~TOTAL, colors = "YlOrRd", size = ~TOTAL, sizes = c(5, 30), type = "scatterternary", mode = "markers", text = ~paste0("Subzone: ", SZ, "
Young: ", scales::percent(YOUNG / TOTAL, 0.1), "
Active: ", scales::percent(ACTIVE / TOTAL, 0.1), "
Old: ", scales::percent(OLD / TOTAL, 0.1), "
Total: ", formatC(TOTAL, format = "d", big.mark = ",")), hoverinfo = "text" ) %>% layout( title = "Population Structure by Subzone, Singapore 2018", ternary = ternaryAxes, coloraxis = list(colorscale = "YlOrRd", colorbar = list(title = "Total Population")) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive ternary plot — colour encodes total population ::: ::: --- ## Summary | Approach | Package | Interactivity | Best for | |----------|---------|---------------|----------| | `ggtern()` + `geom_point()` | `ggtern` | Static | Reports, print | | `ggtern()` + `theme_rgbw()` | `ggtern` | Static | Styled static output | | `plot_ly()` scatterternary | `plotly` | Interactive | Web / HTML output | **Key takeaways:** - Ternary plots are ideal when three proportions must sum to 1 (or 100%). - `ggtern` extends the familiar `ggplot2` API — most `geom_*` and `theme_*` functions still work. - `plotly`'s `scatterternary` type adds hover tooltips and zoom for exploratory analysis. - Encoding an additional variable (colour, size) can reveal structure that a plain ternary plot obscures.
